title: Spotted Dick
subtitle: Suet sponge with dried currants, steamed in a basin or baked in the oven
excerpt: The great British suet sponge: plain flour, shredded suet, caster sugar, dried currants, milk, and an egg, mixed and steamed until the crumb is soft and the currants have swollen. Served warm with custard or crème anglaise.
sourceNotes: Spotted dick appears in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) as a variation on the suet pudding canon. The name refers to the currants spotted through the pale suet sponge. The recipe here follows the proportions and method from mid-Victorian British cookery writing.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooled pudding in the basin, wrapped in foil, for up to two months. Steam from frozen for 1 hour 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before, cool in the basin, and refrigerate. Re-steam for 30 minutes before serving.

content.text: Suet puddings make no apologies. The fat is coarse and solid, the mixing is done by hand, and the result is one of the most satisfying British desserts: a dense, moist sponge with swollen currants throughout, lifted only by the custard poured alongside. The recipe has barely changed since the mid-Victorian period, and there is no reason to change it now.

Steaming is the traditional method and gives the better result, the pudding stays moist and never risks drying at the edges. If you don't have a steamer, a large saucepan with a trivet or upturned saucer on the base works perfectly. The foil covering must be tightly pleated to allow for the pudding to expand as it cooks.

items.prepNote: plus a little more if needed

content.text: A 1.2-litre pudding basin. Butter it generously and line the base with a circle of baking paper.

Combine {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, {{suet-shredded}} of shredded suet, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and {{dried-currants}} of dried currants in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre. Beat the egg into {{whole-milk}} of milk and pour into the well. Mix to a soft, dropping consistency, it should drop from the spoon in 3 seconds. Add a little more milk if the mixture feels stiff.

Spoon into the prepared basin and level the surface. Cut a double layer of foil large enough to cover the top and overhang by 5 cm. Make a pleat across the centre of the foil (this allows the pudding to rise). Press the foil over the rim of the basin and tie firmly under the lip with kitchen string, leaving a handle of string to lower and lift the basin.

Place the basin on a trivet or upturned saucer in a large saucepan. Pour in boiling water to come halfway up the sides of the basin. Bring to a steady simmer, cover the pan, and steam for 1 hour 30 minutes. Check the water level every 30 minutes; top up with boiling water as needed. The pudding is ready when a skewer inserted through the foil into the centre comes out clean.

Lift the basin from the water, remove the foil, and rest for 5 minutes. Run a palette knife around the edge, place a warmed plate over the basin, and invert. The pudding should drop out cleanly. Serve with warm custard or crème anglaise.

items.symptom: Pudding is dense and gummy at the centre
items.cause: Understeamed, or the saucepan went off the boil
items.fix: Keep the saucepan at a steady simmer throughout. Test with a skewer at 1 hour 30 minutes and continue steaming in 10-minute intervals if the skewer comes out wet.

items.cause: Basin wasn't well greased, or the baking-paper circle was omitted from the base
items.fix: Run a palette knife around the full edge before inverting. A 10-second blast from a blowtorch on the outside of the basin can release a stubborn pudding.

items.symptom: Foil seal blew off during steaming
items.cause: String wasn't tied tightly enough, or the pleat was too small to allow for rising
items.fix: Use a large enough foil sheet (30 cm across) with a generous pleat. Tie the string firmly under the rim of the basin, not over the foil.

items.symptom: Pudding is pale and rubbery rather than golden-brown
items.cause: Oven-baked instead of steamed, or steaming time was too short
items.fix: Steaming gives a paler exterior than oven baking, this is correct for a suet pudding. A golden-brown surface means the pudding was baked, not steamed.

content.text: The spotted suet sponge is one of the oldest continuous recipes in British cooking. The name appears in Mrs Beeton's 1861 household management manual, though the pudding is certainly older, belonging to the broad Victorian tradition of economical steamed sweets that stretched kitchen fat across many servings. The currants, the spots, were dried Zante currants, not the fresh blackcurrant or redcurrant that can confuse the name. The dish survives in school canteens, hospital kitchens, and a handful of old-fashioned restaurants, and it repays making at home far more than its reputation suggests.
